attributes.stem:
A 69-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a lump in her neck that has been present for many years and is slowly increasing in size. She reports no dysphagia and no neck pain, however she mentions that she has been getting increasingly tired. Her medical history includes rheumatoid arthritis, heart failure, diabetes mellitus type 2, urinary incontinence, and hypertension. Her regular medications are methotrexate, furosemide, metformin, and amlodipine.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 130/90 mmHg, respiratory rate 15/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Apart from the neck swelling, the physical examination is otherwise normal.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would most strongly suggest that the swelling needs prompt treatment?
options: Evidence of pressure necrosis of the overlying skin
Gurgling in the neck on swallowing
Hoarseness of the voice towards the end of the day
Difficulty in breathing on lying down
Dizziness when she turns her head
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a large goitre. It is essential to perform a thorough airway assessment on patients presenting with a goitre to evaluate for any signs of airway obstruction. If airway obstruction is present, then urgent intervention will be needed. Any problems with breathing should alert both the clinician and the patient to the urgency of the situation. Inspiratory stridor requires immediate referral and intervention.

The most concerning feature in this case would be difficulty in breathing on lying down. This symptom suggests possible airway compression by the goitre, which can worsen when the patient is supine due to the effect of gravity on the enlarged thyroid gland. In such instances, an urgent referral to thyroid surgeons should be made for consideration of thyroidectomy. Other indications for thyroidectomy in this case might include thyrotoxicosis, cosmetic reasons, and suspected malignancy.

Dizziness when she turns her head is not a specific sign of airway obstruction. Dizziness may also be caused by vertigo or hypotension, which are not directly related to the goitre.

Hoarseness of the voice towards the end of the day does not indicate urgent treatment for a large goitre. Voice hoarseness due to a large goitre typically presents as persistent voice hoarseness throughout the day, not just at the end of the day. It's also important to note that voice hoarseness is a common finding in older patients with a goitre. If the patient is comfortable and does not present with any other symptoms which reduce their quality of life, the decision to await surgery is reasonable, even if hoarseness is present, especially in an older patient with several comorbidities.

Gurgling in the neck on swallowing is not a compressive symptom and does not necessarily indicate a need for urgent intervention.

Evidence of pressure necrosis of the overlying skin is an unlikely complication of a large goitre. While it might indicate a very large or rapidly growing mass, it is not typically associated with airway compromise. Pressure necrosis would suggest a different type of pathology, such as a rapidly growing malignancy, but it is not directly related to the immediate risk of airway obstruction. Therefore, while it may require evaluation, it typically does not necessitate immediate management.

In summary, difficulty breathing when lying down is the most concerning symptom among the options provided, as it suggests possible airway obstruction. This symptom warrants prompt evaluation and potential surgical intervention to prevent further airway compromise.
learningPoints: Airway assessment should be done for patients presenting with a large goitre.
Difficulty breathing when lying down is a concerning sign in patients with goitre, suggesting possible airway obstruction.
Indications for thyroidectomy in goitre include airway obstruction, thyrotoxicosis, cosmetic reasons, and suspected malignancy.
references: Wang T, Lyden M, Sosa J. Thyroidectomy. In: UptoDate [online]. 2021. Cited 2023 Jan 1. Available at: https://www.uptodate.com/contents/thyroidectomy

Bobanga ID, McHenry CR. Surgical Procedures. Thyroidectomy: Indications, Flexible Laryngoscopy, Operative Techniques, Recurrent Laryngeal Nerve Monitoring, and Management of Complications. In: Shifrin AL, Raffaelli M, Randolph GW, Gimm O, editors. Endocrine Surgery Comprehensive Board Exam Guide. Cham: Springer International Publishing; 2021:217-45.  Available at: https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-030-84737-1_10
